warnings: Only 4 of 21 affiliate-owned units have full P&L disclosure; remaining units show gross sales only.
Franchisor has a short operating history (franchising since 2019) and the Special Risks section flags financial condition concerns.

extracted.brandBackground: Chicago-origin ice cream brand established in 1926, acquired by affiliate Rainbow Cone LLC in 2018, now franchising three formats (Free Standing, In-Line, Express) plus Food Truck add-on.
extracted.conceptRationale: Quick-service ice cream shop selling cones, cakes, sundaes, shakes, and related products with counter/drive-through service and no full table-service dining.
extracted.staffingRationale: Item 15 requires a full-time Operating Manager (40+ hrs/week) plus crew; Item 19 P&L shows substantial salaries and wages line items consistent with a conventionally staffed QSR.

leadership.name: Joseph Buonavolanto Jr.
leadership.background: Co-Manager of Five Flavors Franchising LLC since 2018; long-tenured Co-Manager of The Buona Companies and Buona Beef entities since 1999.
leadership.whyItMatters: Founding manager with decades of multi-unit restaurant operating experience backing the system.

leadership.name: Joseph Buonavolanto III
leadership.background: EVP since January 2024; previously VP of Franchising from 2022; prior Director of Operations for Buona Beef LLC.
leadership.whyItMatters: Primary franchise development and support executive driving franchisee growth.

leadership.name: Mike Buonavolanto
leadership.role: Manager and Vice President of Real Estate
leadership.background: Manager of Five Flavors Franchising LLC since 2018; Director of Real Estate for The Buona Companies since 2014.
leadership.whyItMatters: Controls site selection and real estate approval process critical to franchisee location success.

leadership.name: Nick Arajuo
leadership.background: CFO of The Buona Companies LLC since 2019; Director of Finance from 2001–2019.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees financial reporting and controls for the franchisor and affiliated entities.

leadership.name: Laurie Cairns
leadership.background: SVP of Marketing for The Buona Companies LLC since September 2024; previously self-employed at Cairns Communications.
leadership.whyItMatters: Leads brand marketing fund strategy and franchisee advertising programs.

leadership.name: Brie Craft
leadership.background: Learning and Development Manager since May 2024; Franchise Onboarding and Training Manager from 2022–2024 at The Buona Companies.
leadership.whyItMatters: Manages initial and ongoing franchisee training programs.

item19.notes: Item 19 covers 21 affiliate-owned units and 5 franchised units for the period March 24, 2025 to March 24, 2026. Full P&L is provided for 4 affiliate Free Standing units and 1 affiliate Express stand-alone unit; all other units show gross sales only. Affiliate units do not pay franchisor fees, but fees are imputed in the P&L tables. Franchised unit data is gross sales from POS only — no P&L. Data is unaudited.

hiddenCosts.name: Required Software and Applications (Monthly)
hiddenCosts.description: Estimated $1,000/month for required third-party software and applications (online ordering, loyalty, delivery, inventory, labor, accounting, security) paid directly to providers.

hiddenCosts.name: PCI DSS Compliance
hiddenCosts.description: Estimated $125/month for payment card industry data security standards compliance.

hiddenCosts.name: POS and Computer System Annual Maintenance
hiddenCosts.description: Estimated $1,200/year to maintain POS and computer systems.

hiddenCosts.name: Mandatory Affiliate Architect/Design Fee (Deposit)
hiddenCosts.description: $5,000 non-refundable deposit to affiliate Keystone Planning + Design PLLC required at site approval; total design fees $3,500–$60,000 depending on format.

hiddenCosts.name: Supplier Rebates to Franchisor
hiddenCosts.description: Approved suppliers pay rebates to franchisor based on franchisee purchase volume; amount not disclosed.

hiddenCosts.name: Customer Satisfaction Reimbursement
hiddenCosts.description: Franchisee must reimburse franchisor for any customer refunds issued at franchisor's sole discretion; amount varies.

hiddenCosts.name: Non-Compliance Fee
hiddenCosts.description: $100–$1,000 per day from first date of default until cured, depending on nature of default.

hiddenCosts.name: Supplemental / Additional Training Fees
hiddenCosts.description: Up to 10 mandatory training days per year at $300/day per trainer plus travel; tuition for supplemental seminars not yet determined.

hiddenCosts.name: Re-inspection Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Franchisee reimburses franchisor's actual costs for a second quality-assurance inspection if deficiencies are found.

hiddenCosts.name: Mandatory Remodel at Renewal
hiddenCosts.description: Franchisee must remodel, reimage, renovate, and modernize to then-current standards within 6 months of signing renewal agreement; cost not specified.

operationalRisks.title: Franchisor Financial Condition Risk
operationalRisks.description: The Special Risks section explicitly states the franchisor's financial condition calls into question its ability to provide services and support; financial statements are audited but the front-matter risk disclosure flags this concern.

operationalRisks.title: Short Operating History
operationalRisks.description: Franchisor began offering franchises in 2019 and is at an early stage of development, making this a riskier investment than a more established system.

operationalRisks.title: Spousal Liability Guarantee
operationalRisks.description: Franchisee's spouse must sign a guarantee making them personally liable for all financial obligations under the Franchise Agreement, placing marital and personal assets at risk.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: All disputes must be arbitrated or litigated in Illinois, which may increase costs and reduce leverage for franchisees located in other states.

operationalRisks.title: Seasonal Revenue Concentration
operationalRisks.description: Ice cream sales are highly seasonal; several affiliate units are only open May–September, creating significant cash flow risk during winter months.

operationalRisks.title: Affiliate Competition Within Protected Area
operationalRisks.description: Franchisor retains the right to establish Express franchises at captive sites (airports, malls, stadiums, etc.) within the franchisee's Protected Area without compensation.

operationalRisks.title: Mandatory Affiliate Supplier for Architecture
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must use affiliate Keystone Planning + Design PLLC as the sole designated architect, limiting competitive bidding on design costs.

operationalRisks.title: High Termination Rate in 2025
operationalRisks.description: Three franchised outlets were terminated in 2025 against only three openings, resulting in zero net franchised unit growth for the year.

operationalRisks.title: Liquidated Damages on Early Termination
operationalRisks.description: If franchisee is terminated for material breach or abandons the business, franchisor may collect a lump-sum equal to the net present value of all remaining royalties for the unexpired term.

operationalRisks.title: No Financing Offered
operationalRisks.description: Franchisor provides no direct or indirect financing; franchisees must secure all capital independently, which is challenging given the franchisor's limited track record.

rentDetail.source: Item 7 discloses lease deposits and 3-month rent payments but does not state a per-sqft or monthly rent rate; Item 11 notes franchisor will not approve a site with estimated occupancy cost exceeding 12% of projected AUV (Item 11, p.39). No specific $/sqft rent rate is disclosed.

riskReasons: Capital gap of $2,339,366 is ~9x your liquid — extreme leverage; the deal rests almost entirely on outside capital.
5 high-severity operational tripwires disclosed — see the tripwires section.
Franchisor financial distress disclosed (negative net worth) — confirm its ability to support the system against the audited statements.
Franchisee unit economics could not be scored — no usable Item 19 (company/affiliate-owned only, or no FPR). Treat this as UNVERIFIED, not low-risk.
notes: Average rent not found; fixed costs may be understated.
No Item 19 middle-cohort or network-average revenue found; economics are indeterminate.

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $2,589,366 mid-point build-out, there is a $2,339,366 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal.

insights.conceptRationale: Quick-service ice cream shop selling cones, cakes, sundaes, shakes, and related products with counter/drive-through service and no full table-service dining.

benchmark.label: QSR / fast-casual
benchmark.dominantRisk: Prime cost and throughput per labor hour. Smaller footprint helps fixed costs, but COGS + labor still set the ceiling.
considerations: Budget COGS ~28–33% and labor ~22–28%; target prime cost under ~60%.
Verify average ticket and daily transaction counts with Item 20 operators, not the franchisor.
Watch for required remodels and equipment refresh cycles buried in Item 6/Item 11.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 10–18% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

assumptions.detail: Classified as “QSR / fast-casual” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: Several stress signals show up together here — a net-worth deficit of roughly $322K alongside operating losses, thin near-term liquidity, and unit closures. Any one of these alone can be normal for a young brand; appearing together is the pattern that separates real distress from ordinary early-stage losses. Treat this as a franchisor-stability question to resolve — with the specific items below — before you commit.
body: What we found (2025-12-30, audited by R.J. Augustine & Associates, Ltd.): Net loss of $234K on $509K of revenue — roughly 1.5x spent per $1 earned. Losses are widening, not shrinking. Negative net worth (a members'/stockholders' deficit) of $322K, and deepening year over year. Total liabilities are about 1.4x total assets. About 50% of liabilities is money owed to related parties — the entity is kept alive largely by insider loans, not profit. Cash of $486K against $221K of obligations due within a year (a current ratio of 2.37). Note: about 35% of those liabilities is deferred revenue — prepaid franchise fees the company owes as future services, not borrowed money. Netting it out, liabilities are still about 0.9x assets.
Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: negative net worth that is deepening year over year
heavy dependence on related-party loans to stay funded
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
